name: codebase-cleanup-refactor-clean
description: You are a code refactoring expert specializing in clean code principles, SOLID design patterns, and modern software engineering best practices. Analyze and refactor the provided code to improve its quality, maintainability, and performance.
capabilities: Analyzes code for SOLID violations, code smells, and performance bottlenecks
Applies refactoring patterns including Strategy, Factory, Observer, and Repository
Breaks down monolithic functions into single-responsibility methods under 20 lines
Generates comprehensive test suites achieving 80%+ coverage for refactored code
Provides before/after comparisons with complexity metrics and performance improvements
Creates migration guides for transitioning from legacy to modern architectures
author: sickn33
securitySummary: All 89 static analysis findings are false positives. The skill contains only markdown documentation with code examples for educational purposes. Detected patterns (backticks, file operations, network references) are part of fenced code blocks demonstrating refactoring concepts in Python, TypeScript, Go, and Java. No executable code, prompt injection attempts, or malicious patterns found. Safe for publication.
valueStatement: Struggling with technical debt, duplicated code, and hard-to-maintain systems? This skill applies proven refactoring patterns and SOLID principles to transform messy codebases into clean, modular, testable architectures.
userTitle: Refactor Code to Clean, SOLID Architecture
limitations: Cannot refactor code without reading the actual source files first
Does not execute build commands or run test suites automatically
Requires existing test coverage to validate refactoring safety
Large-scale architectural changes may need multiple iterations
Cannot guarantee preservation of undocumented business logic

useCases.title: Legacy Monolith Modernization
useCases.description: Transform 500-line monolithic functions into clean, modular architecture with separated concerns for validation, business logic, and data access.

useCases.targetUser: Tech lead planning quarterly refactoring sprint
useCases.title: Technical Debt Cleanup Sprint
useCases.description: Systematically eliminate code duplication, extract magic numbers to constants, and apply SOLID principles across high-risk, frequently-modified modules.

useCases.title: Pre-Feature Architecture Review
useCases.description: Evaluate codebase quality before major feature work, identifying refactoring opportunities that would reduce implementation risk and improve extensibility.

promptTemplates.title: Quick Code Cleanup
promptTemplates.prompt: Review the current codebase for code smells and quick wins. Identify magic numbers, improve variable names, remove dead code, and extract any duplicate code into reusable functions.

promptTemplates.title: SOLID Principles Refactoring
promptTemplates.prompt: Analyze this code for SOLID violations. Refactor to follow Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, and Dependency Inversion principles. Show before/after comparisons.

promptTemplates.title: Monolith to Modular Architecture
promptTemplates.prompt: Break down this monolithic system into clean layers: domain entities, repositories, services, and validators. Apply dependency injection and separate concerns completely. Generate comprehensive tests.

promptTemplates.title: Technical Debt Prioritization
promptTemplates.prompt: Audit the entire codebase and prioritize technical debt by business impact and risk. Provide a refactoring roadmap with effort estimates, ROI analysis, and recommended sprint scheduling.

bestPractices: Always read existing code before proposing changes to understand context and business logic
Start with high-impact, low-effort refactorings like extracting magic numbers and improving names
Generate comprehensive tests before refactoring to ensure behavior preservation
Break large refactorings into small, reviewable commits that can be tested independently
antiPatterns: Avoid rewriting entire modules from scratch without understanding existing requirements
Do not apply design patterns everywhere - use them only when they solve actual problems
Never refactor without tests or mechanism to validate functional equivalence
Don't change multiple unrelated concerns in a single refactoring iteration

faq.question: Will this skill modify my code automatically?
faq.answer: No. The skill analyzes code and suggests refactoring strategies. You review the plan, then Claude applies changes with your approval using Edit tools.

faq.question: What types of code can this skill refactor?
faq.answer: Works with Python, TypeScript, JavaScript, Go, Java, C#, Ruby, and most mainstream languages. Focuses on structure and design, not language-specific optimizations.

faq.question: How do I know if refactoring is safe?
faq.answer: The skill generates comprehensive tests before refactoring and validates changes afterward. It also identifies risk factors like low test coverage and recommends conservative approaches.

faq.question: Can this skill fix performance issues?
faq.answer: Partially. It identifies algorithmic inefficiencies like O(n²) operations and suggests caching strategies. However, it does not replace dedicated performance profiling tools.

faq.question: What if my codebase has no tests?
faq.answer: The skill will first recommend adding test coverage before refactoring. It can generate characterization tests for existing behavior, then proceed with conservative refactorings.

faq.question: How long does a typical refactoring take?
faq.answer: Varies by scope. Quick cleanup (variable names, dead code) takes minutes. SOLID refactoring for a class takes 30-60 minutes. Major architectural restructuring may require multiple sessions across days.
